Your symptoms (clear runny/stuffy nose, frequent sneezing, itchy/watery eyes that worsen outdoors and in the mornings, no fever or sore throat) fit allergic rhinitis (“seasonal allergies”) more than an infection. This is usually safe to manage at home first with over-the-counter treatment. Consider a daily non-drowsy antihistamine (cetirizine, loratadine, or fexofenadine), a daily steroid nasal spray (fluticasone or triamcinolone—often the most effective, used consistently for 1–2+ weeks), saline rinses, and reducing pollen exposure (shower/change clothes after being outside, keep windows closed, use HEPA filter). See a primary care doctor or allergist if symptoms aren’t improving after ~2–4 weeks of consistent OTC treatment, are significantly affecting sleep/work, or if you develop wheezing/shortness of breath, facial pain/fever, thick foul-smelling discharge, or swelling around the eyes (those would warrant sooner evaluation).

Your symptoms (clear runny/stuffy nose, frequent sneezing, itchy/watery eyes, worse outdoors and in the morning, no fever or sore throat) fit allergic rhinitis (“hay fever”), which is common after moving to a new area with different pollens. You can usually start with OTC treatment: a daily steroid nasal spray (fluticasone or triamcinolone) used consistently for 1–2 weeks, a non-drowsy oral antihistamine (cetirizine, loratadine, or fexofenadine) as needed, saline nasal rinse, and antihistamine eye drops (ketotifen) for itchy eyes. Consider seeing your primary care doctor or an allergist if symptoms aren’t well-controlled after 2–4 weeks of consistent OTC therapy, if you need medicines daily for months, or if you develop wheezing/shortness of breath, significant facial pain/fever, thick foul-smelling discharge, or symptoms mainly on one side.

Your symptoms (clear runny/stuffy nose, sneezing, itchy/watery eyes worse outdoors/mornings, no fever or sore throat, history of eczema) fit very well with seasonal/environmental allergies (allergic rhinitis), which are common in Austin. This usually can be managed at home with OTC options: a daily non-drowsy antihistamine (cetirizine, loratadine, or fexofenadine) and/or a daily nasal steroid spray (fluticasone or triamcinolone) used consistently for 1–2 weeks; saline rinses can also help. You should see a doctor (or allergist) if symptoms aren’t improving after a couple weeks of consistent OTC treatment, if you develop wheezing/shortness of breath, frequent sinus infections, significant facial pain/pressure, high fever, or if symptoms are disrupting sleep despite treatment.

Your symptoms (burning with urination, frequent urge with little output, and lower belly discomfort for 2 days) are very consistent with an uncomplicated bladder infection (UTI). These usually don’t reliably go away on their own and often need antibiotics to prevent worsening or spreading to the kidneys. You don’t have emergency warning signs right now (no fever, flank/back pain, vomiting, or blood in urine), but you should get evaluated and treated within the next 24 hours—this can often be done through a same-day clinic, urgent care, or telehealth. Many telehealth services can prescribe antibiotics after reviewing symptoms and sometimes arranging a urine test (lab drop-off or pharmacy/clinic test), but it’s safest not to self-treat with leftover antibiotics.

Your symptoms (burning behind the breastbone/upper stomach after meals or when lying down, sour taste, partial relief with Tums) fit typical acid reflux/heartburn (GERD), and you don’t report alarm signs like trouble swallowing, vomiting blood, black stools, or unexplained weight loss.
